title: Proverbs 31
subtitle: A Mother's Instruction to a King; The Excellent Wife: The Book's Final Word

sections.header: Verses 1–9 — A Mother's Instruction to a King: Speak Up

heb.paragraph: The words of King Lemuel — the maśśāʾ his mother taught him (v. 1). Maśśāʾ can mean either a prophetic oracle or a place-name (Massa, an Arabian tribe). Either way, the instruction comes from a mother to a king — a remarkable authority claim. A queen mother shapes royal policy through wisdom, warning against women who ruin kings and wine that clouds judgment.

Open your mouth (pitḥû-pîkā) for the mute, for the rights of all the destitute (v. 8). The command to speak for those who cannot speak for themselves is the ethical climax of the queen mother's instruction. Royal power exists not for self-aggrandisement but for advocacy. The bĕnê ḥălôp (sons of passing away, i.e. the perishing) are those whose voices the powerful must amplify.

panels.ctx: Proverbs 31 opens with the sayings of King Lemuel — another non-Israelite wisdom figure, like Agur. Uniquely, this instruction was taught to him by his mother. The instruction for a king is brief and pointed: avoid women who ruin kings, avoid wine that clouds royal judgment, and speak up for the poor and destitute. V.8–9 are among the most activist verses in Proverbs: "Speak up for those who cannot speak for themselves." The book that began with a father's instruction to a son ends with a mother's instruction to a king and a poem about a woman.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: A mother's instruction -- Alter notes the unique framing: this is the one passage in the entire collection where wisdom is explicitly transmitted from a mother to a son. The content matches: defend the vulnerable, avoid the temptations of power.

Speak up for those who cannot speak -- Alter notes the verse is the ethical climax of the king's instruction. The king's power is specifically for advocacy of the powerless. Power that does not serve the vulnerable has missed its covenant purpose.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: A mother's instruction to King Lemuel is Proverbs' only explicitly matrilineal wisdom. Waltke notes the attribution to the king's mother makes this passage unique: the teacher is a woman, the student is a king, and the subject is the responsibilities of power. The mother's warnings -- against women, wine, and the neglect of justice -- are the three primary temptations of kingship.

Speak up for those who cannot speak for themselves; defend the rights of the poor and needy -- Waltke notes this is the climactic instruction of the mother's teaching. The king's unique obligation is advocacy for the voiceless. The conclusion of the mother's instruction is not a personal virtue but a social responsibility.

Calvin: The oracle that his mother taught him -- this teaches that the education of rulers belongs especially to women, since they have the most immediate formative influence. The instruction of kings begins in the household.

Calvin: Defend the rights of the poor and needy -- this is the primary covenant obligation of the king. All the other warnings (against women and wine) serve this positive purpose: the king must be unimpaired to fulfil his justice obligation.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: King Lemuel -- his identity is unknown. The note discusses whether Lemuel is another name for Solomon or an unknown king. The oracle (massa) may also be a place name (Massa in Arabia), suggesting this wisdom came from outside Israel.

NET: pen lol benei chalof (literally 'those who are passing away' -- the vulnerable dying) -- the note explains the specific social groups the king is to defend: those who cannot speak for themselves in the legal system.

sections.header: Verses 10–31 — The Excellent Wife: The Book's Final Answer

heb.paragraph: The ʾēšet ḥayil poem (vv. 10–31) is an acrostic: each verse begins with the next letter of the Hebrew alphabet, signalling completeness. Ḥayil means strength, capability, and worth — the same word describes warriors (gibbôr ḥayil). This woman is not decorative but formidable: she trades, plants, manufactures, and provides. She is Lady Wisdom incarnate in daily life.

Charm is deceptive and beauty is fleeting, but a woman who fears the LORD (yirʾat yhwh) is to be praised (v. 30). The book's final verdict circles back to its first principle: yirʾat yhwh, the fear of the LORD that was declared 'the beginning of knowledge' in 1:7. The entire arc of Proverbs — from the naive youth of chapter 1 to the accomplished woman of chapter 31 — is framed by this single phrase.

Give her of the fruit of her hands, and let her works halĕlûhā — praise her — in the gates (v. 31). The verb hālal (to praise) is the root of 'hallelujah.' The city gates were the place of public honour and legal recognition. The book closes not with abstract theology but with concrete recognition: wisdom lived out in a woman's daily work receives public, lasting praise.

panels.ctx: The poem of the excellent wife (vv.10–31) is an acrostic — each verse begins with the next letter of the Hebrew alphabet, from aleph to taw. This is not merely a literary flourish; it signals completeness. The A-to-Z of human excellence is embodied in this woman. She is the answer to the question with which the book wrestled: what does wisdom look like when fully embodied in a human life? The answer is not a philosopher or a king but a woman whose fear of the LORD produces comprehensive excellence in every dimension of her life.

cross.note: The book began with "the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom" — it ends with "a woman who fears the LORD is to be praised." The thesis and the embodiment bracket the entire collection.
Ruth is identified as an ʾēšet ḥayil in Ruth 3:11 — "all the people of my town know that you are a woman of noble character." Ruth is the narrative embodiment of the Prov 31 portrait.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The eshet chayil acrostic -- Alter notes the alphabetic acrostic is a form that performs completeness. The poem covers the entire Hebrew alphabet from aleph to taw -- the entire range of possible virtue is expressed. The woman is described as strong (chayil), productive, generous, wise, and God-fearing.

Charm is deceptive and beauty is fleeting -- Alter notes the poem's closing verses explicitly address appearance vs. reality. The woman praised is not the conventionally beautiful woman but the woman whose life is characterised by the fear of the LORD. The poem's final verdict is theological.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The eshet chayil poem is Proverbs' concluding statement and one of the Hebrew Bible's most complete literary achievements. Waltke notes the acrostic structure (each verse beginning with successive letters of the Hebrew alphabet) gives the poem a quality of completeness and order -- the whole alphabet, the whole woman, the complete expression of covenant wisdom in embodied feminine form.

Charm is deceptive and beauty is fleeting, but a woman who fears the LORD is to be praised -- Waltke notes the climax of the eshet chayil poem is the fear of the LORD. The entire book began with the fear of the LORD (1:7); it ends with the woman whose life is characterised by it. The fear of the LORD that was the book's epistemological thesis is now the book's final portrait: it looks like this woman.

Calvin: Who can find a virtuous woman? This question is not pessimistic but rhetorical -- she is rare and therefore precious. The poem is the portrait of the ideal covenant woman, offered not as an impossible standard but as an inspiring goal.

Calvin: The fear of the LORD is the supreme feminine virtue -- and indeed the supreme human virtue. The poem ends where the book began: with the fear of the LORD as the source and standard of all genuine excellence.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: eshet chayil -- the note connects to Boaz's recognition of Ruth as eshet chayil (Ruth 3:11) and the earlier description of Boaz himself as ish gibbor chayil (Ruth 2:1). Chayil (strength, valor, excellence) is not merely domestic but encompasses military, moral, economic, and relational capacity.

NET: the fear of the LORD as the poem's theological climax -- the note traces the inclusio with 1:7 (the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom) and 9:10 (the beginning of wisdom is the fear of the LORD). The book's thesis in ch.1 is the book's final portrait in ch.31.

ppl.name: Lemuel's mother
ppl.text: The only woman explicitly named as a wisdom teacher in the book. Her instruction to her son is direct, practical, and politically courageous.

ppl.name: The excellent wife
ppl.role: The book's final embodiment of wisdom
ppl.text: Not an ideal but a portrait: fear of the LORD expressed in every dimension of life — commercial, domestic, philanthropic, pedagogical.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"Charm is deceptive, and beauty is fleeting; but a woman who fears the LORD is to be praised."NoteThe verse is the poem's theological climax and the book's final anchor. The acrostic poem's A-to-Z of excellence culminates not in achievement but in relationship with God.

src.title: Acrostic Poem
src.quote: The Hebrew acrostic (each verse beginning with successive letters of the alphabet, aleph to taw) signals completeness. Hebrew acrostics appear in Ps 119 (the longest), Lamentations 1–4, and Pss 9–10, 25, 34, 37. In each case the form communicates totality — the full range of a subject.
src.note: The acrostic form makes a claim about completeness: this is wisdom embodied A to Z.

src.title: Lady Wisdom Embodied
src.quote: Scholars have noted that the excellent wife (ʾēšet ḥayil) is the human embodiment of Lady Wisdom (chs.1–9). Both are praised; both are worth more than rubies; both build their houses; both are found by those who seek them. The poem deliberately completes the Lady Wisdom motif: the personification of wisdom in chs.1–9 finds its human expression in a real woman.
src.note: The ʾēšet ḥayil and Lady Wisdom are the two poles of Proverbs' female wisdom portrait.

rec.text: Ruth is explicitly called an ʾēšet ḥayil — the only other person in Scripture to receive this title. Ruth is the narrative of Proverbs 31 lived out: comprehensive excellence rooted in covenant faithfulness.
The excellent wife embodies the Lady Wisdom of ch.8: present, active, life-giving, accessible. The book closes its female wisdom portrait with its final word: fear of the LORD.

rows.label: The Mother's Instruction: Power for the Voiceless
rows.text: Speak up for those who cannot speak for themselves, for the rights of all who are destitute. The king's unique obligation is advocacy for the voiceless. Power that does not serve the vulnerable has missed its covenant purpose.

rows.label: The Fear of the LORD: First and Final Word
rows.text: Charm is deceptive and beauty is fleeting, but a woman who fears the LORD is to be praised. The book that began with the fear of the LORD (1:7) ends with its fullest embodiment. The poem's final verdict is theological.

debate.title: Is the Eshet Chayil Real Woman, Wisdom Personified, or Both?

positions.name: A real ideal woman, not a personification
positions.argument: The poem is a practical portrait of the covenant household woman -- real, active, embodied. It is an ideal but not an abstract one. The poem is meant to produce praise for real women and to shape the expectations of young men choosing a wife.

positions.name: Lady Wisdom in embodied form
positions.argument: The eshet chayil is Lady Wisdom of chs.1-9 now embodied in a domestic portrait. The poem deliberately echoes the description of Wisdom: she is worth more than rubies (3:15 = 31:10); she speaks with wisdom (31:26 = 8:1-36). The poem closes the Wisdom inclusion that ch.1 opened.

positions.name: Both: a real woman who also personifies covenant wisdom
positions.argument: The poem describes a real type of woman AND serves as the embodiment of the Lady Wisdom figure. Both readings are simultaneously valid and mutually reinforcing.
